skill.name: planning-with-files
skill.description: Adds file-based planning workflows for complex agent tasks using task_plan.md, findings.md, progress.md, and helper scripts.
skill.summary: Create persistent planning files so long multi-step AI tasks remain organized and resumable.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged many command, network, browser storage, and weak crypto patterns. Most are false positives from markdown examples, template text, or documentation links. The real concern is that the skill declares Bash access and runs shell hooks, including a stop hook that can influence agent behavior.

medium_findings.title: Shell Hooks Execute During Agent Workflow
medium_findings.description: The skill manifest enables Bash and defines command hooks for session start, pre-tool use, post-tool use, and stop events. The commands are hardcoded and mostly read or report planning status, but automatic shell execution expands the trust boundary for users who install the skill.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The manifest clearly declares Bash access and command hooks. The commands appear purposeful, but automatic shell execution is a real elevated-risk behavior.

medium_findings.title: Stop Hook Can Pressure Continued Execution
medium_findings.description: The stop hook runs a completion checker that exits with failure when phases are incomplete and prints an instruction not to stop. This can conflict with user intent or platform stop behavior, although it does not show malicious exfiltration or code injection.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The behavior is explicit in the stop hook and checker script. The risk is workflow influence, not confirmed malicious control.

low_findings.title: Static Command Alerts Are Mostly Documentation Examples
low_findings.description: Many command detections occur inside fenced markdown examples or inline references to files and tools. These examples are not executed directly by the skill and do not provide a command injection path by themselves.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited locations are markdown examples or conceptual documentation. I did not find evidence that these specific snippets are executed.

low_findings.title: Network Findings Are Documentation References
low_findings.description: The detected hardcoded URLs are a Manus source link and an example Python documentation URL. I did not find evidence that the skill sends data to these URLs.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs appear in documentation and templates only. No request logic or data upload behavior was found at those locations.

low_findings.title: Weak Crypto and Browser Storage Alerts Are False Positives
low_findings.description: Weak cryptography alerts map to headings, markdown filenames, or ordinary planning text rather than cryptographic code. The browser storage alert is an example dark-mode decision that mentions localStorage, not a credential store.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The referenced content is plain markdown and example text. I did not find cryptographic operations or sensitive browser storage access.

dangerous_patterns.title: Automatic Shell Hook Commands
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill registers shell commands that run during session and tool lifecycle events. Hardcoded commands reduce injection risk, but installers should review hook behavior before publishing.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The hook definitions are direct evidence. The commands are simple, so severity remains medium.

dangerous_patterns.title: Project Directory File Creation
dangerous_patterns.description: The initialization script writes planning files into the current project directory when they do not already exist. It avoids overwriting existing files, but it still modifies user workspaces.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The script contains direct file creation logic guarded by existence checks. This is expected behavior for the skill but should be disclosed.

content.user_title: Organize complex tasks with planning files
content.value_statement: Long AI tasks can lose goals, decisions, and errors across many tool calls. This skill creates persistent planning files that keep work organized and easier to resume.
actual_capabilities: Guides agents to create task_plan.md, findings.md, and progress.md in the active project.
Provides templates for phases, decisions, errors, research findings, and progress logs.
Includes shell scripts to initialize planning files and check completion status.
Uses lifecycle hooks to remind agents to review and update planning files.
Documents examples for research, bug fixing, feature work, and error recovery.
limitations: It does not manage tasks outside markdown files.
It depends on agent discipline to keep files current.
The shell hooks may be unsuitable for restricted environments.
It does not verify the quality of plans or findings.

use_cases.title: Plan multi-step engineering work
use_cases.description: Break a feature, bug fix, or refactor into phases with visible status, decisions, and verification notes.

use_cases.title: Preserve research findings
use_cases.description: Capture web, document, and visual findings in a durable file before context becomes too long.

use_cases.title: Resume long agent sessions
use_cases.description: Use progress logs and task plans to restart work after interruptions or context resets.

prompt_templates.title: Start a planning session
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner setup for a complex task
prompt_templates.prompt: Use planning-with-files for this task. Create task_plan.md, findings.md, and progress.md, then start with requirements discovery.

prompt_templates.title: Track implementation progress
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the planning files to manage this feature. Update phase status, decisions, errors, and test results as you work.

prompt_templates.title: Recover from repeated failures
prompt_templates.prompt: Review the plan and progress log. Summarize failed attempts, choose a different approach, and update the error log.

prompt_templates.title: Audit a long research workflow
prompt_templates.prompt: Review task_plan.md, findings.md, and progress.md. Identify missing evidence, unresolved decisions, and final verification steps before delivery.

output_examples.input: Plan a new command-line tool build with several implementation phases.
output_examples.output: A task plan with phased work, a findings file for requirements and decisions, and a progress log for actions and tests.

output_examples.input: Research a topic across web pages and summarize reliable findings.
output_examples.output: A findings document that records sources, key observations, open questions, and a short progress record of research steps.

output_examples.input: Resume a debugging task after many tool calls and failed attempts.
output_examples.output: A refreshed plan showing current phase, previous errors, changed approach, and remaining verification steps.

best_practices: Create planning files before starting tasks with several phases.
Update findings after important searches, document reads, or visual reviews.
Record errors and changed approaches before retrying work.
anti_patterns: Using the skill for simple one-step questions.
Letting generated planning files replace user instructions.
Ignoring shell hook behavior in locked-down environments.

faq.question: What files does this skill create?
faq.answer: It guides creation of task_plan.md, findings.md, and progress.md in the active project directory.

faq.question: Does it overwrite existing planning files?
faq.answer: The init script checks for existing files and skips them instead of overwriting.

faq.question: Does it make network requests?
faq.answer: No network request code was found. The detected URLs are documentation and template examples.

faq.question: Why is the risk level medium?
faq.answer: The skill registers shell hooks and requests Bash access. The commands appear benign but need user awareness.

faq.question: Can it be used with Claude Code and Codex?
faq.answer: The report marks support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.

faq.question: When should I avoid this skill?
faq.answer: Avoid it for quick answers, single-file edits, or environments that prohibit shell hooks.
